content:
3 min read
1 hour ago

--

A static, open-source Flutter web app that opens scripture in one tap. No login, no account, no database sitting around waiting to be breached.
Most apps want a relationship. You sign up, you log in, and a server somewhere starts keeping a little file on you. That file is convenient right up until the morning you get the email that begins, “We recently became aware of an incident affecting your account.” For a shopping app, that is annoying. For the app you used to read scripture, it lands differently.
So I built a Quran reader on the opposite premise: there is no server to remember you, because there is no server at all. It lives at https://p32929.github.io/quran_flutter, and the address is the whole confession. That .github.io means it is a static site, a bundle of files served straight off GitHub Pages. There is no login screen because there is nothing to log into. There is no “your account” because no account exists. It cannot sell your reading habits, cannot email you a re-engagement campaign, and cannot lock Surah Al-Kahf behind a paywall. Not because I am a saint, but because the architecture makes all of that impossible. You cannot leak a database you never built.
I would love to dress this up as a grand privacy philosophy. Honestly, it started as laziness with good consequences. I did not want to run a backend. Backends cost money, need maintenance, and slowly turn into the exact thing I was trying to avoid. A static Flutter web app sidesteps the entire category of problem.
What it actually does
It opens the Quran. That is the headline, and in 2026 it somehow qualifies as a feature. Tap the link and you land on a clean index of all 114 Surahs. No onboarding tour, no permission prompts, no banner ad breathing between you and the first ayah. Pick a Surah and it opens to the text, laid out to be read: Arabic with translation, comfortable typography, and bilingual support so you can keep the meaning beside the original.
The part I am quietly proud of is the search. Instead of scrolling like an archaeologist hunting for a half-remembered verse, you type a keyword and it surfaces the ayat that match. There is recitation audio for when you would rather listen than read, bookmarks for the verses you keep returning to, and a dark mode that does not detonate at 2 a.m. None of it asks who you are. The app’s entire memory of you resets the moment you close the tab, which is exactly how a book should behave.
Why I am posting this
Not because it is a technical marvel. It is deliberately simple, and “I avoided building a backend” is not a Nobel-worthy engineering decision. I am posting it because the absence of all that machinery is the feature. The next time a scripture app demands a signup before showing you a single word, remember that it did not have to. This one is proof.
It is free, open-source, and runs entirely in your browser: https://p32929.github.io/quran_flutter . Open it, read it, fork it, or check the code to confirm I am not lying about any of the above. No account required, because there is nowhere to make one.
